reasoning: The Chicago Fed's National Financial Conditions Index (NFCI) has shown exceptionally strong and accelerating upward momentum over the last 6 weeks, moving from -0.507 to -0.444 (indicating tightening conditions). Given the smooth nature of the dynamic factor model underlying this index, such strong short-term trends very rarely reverse sharply in a single week without an exogenous shock. The upcoming March FOMC meeting is widely expected to hold rates steady, which is a hawkish stance that typically supports tightening financial conditions. Therefore, the short-term probability of the index continuing to increase (being higher than the March 14 baseline by March 23 and April 15) is quite elevated. Over longer horizons (June, September, and March 2026), uncertainty naturally rises. The index tends to exhibit multi-month cyclical swings but generally mean-reverts. With the current value (-0.444) slightly below the 1-year historical average (which ranges roughly between -0.36 and -0.50), the long-term probability of being above this reading decays toward a near-coin-flip 50-55% range. I have cascaded uncertainty outwards toward 0.51 as the horizon extends to 1 year ahead.
